1: Zophar the Naamathite is the least developed of the three Friends biographically; Naamah may be in northwestern Arabia (cf. Joshua 15:41) or is unidentified. His is the shortest first speech (20 verses) and the shortest second speech (Job 20:29); he alone has no third speech in the Masoretic text. His style is the most blunt and accusatory of the three.
2: hărōḇ dᵉḇārîm lōʾ yēʿāneh wᵉʾîm-śᵉpāṯayim yiṣdaq — “should this flood of words go unanswered? Should a man of endless talk be counted righteous?” Rōḇ dᵉḇārîm (multitude of words) — the critique of verbosity as a moral indicator; cf. Ecclesiastes 5:2-3 (let your words be few; many words come with dreams and folly). The sarcasm is that quantity of speech is being conflated with quality of argument: Zophar dismisses Job’s words by volume, not content.
3: baddêḵā mᵉtîm yaḥᵃrîšû wattilaʿ wᵉʾên maklîm — “shall your boasting silence everyone? When you scoff, will no one rebuke you?” Baddêḵā (your boastings/empty claims) — the plural of baḏ (linen/empty boast) — the Friends are silenced not because they lack arguments but because Job’s lament has overpowered their formulaic responses. Zophar frames this as a deficit: someone should be speaking back against Job’s scoffing.
4: kî tōmar zak leqqāḥî wᵉḇar hāyîṯî bᵉʿêneḵā — “for you have said, ‘My doctrine is right and I am clean in God’s sight.’” Leqqāḥî (my doctrine/reception) — from lāqaḥ (to take/receive); the word implies received teaching, traditional doctrine accepted and carried. Zophar puts in Job’s mouth a doctrinal claim Job has not made in these terms; Job has claimed innocence of behavior, not doctrinal infallibility. This misrepresentation is part of the Friends’ pattern.
5: wᵉʾûlam mî-yittēn ʾĕlôhah dabbēr wᵉyipṯaḥ śᵉpāṯāyw ʿimmāḵ — “if only God would speak and open his lips to answer you.” The irony is enormous: Zophar wishes God would speak, apparently confident that God would vindicate his (Zophar’s) position; God will speak (chapters 38-41), but in vindication of Job, not of Zophar. The Friends’ call for divine intervention backfires.
6: wᵉyaggēḏ lᵉḵā taʿᵃlumôṯ ḥokmāh kî-kiplayim lᵉṯûšiyyāh — “and reveal to you the secrets of wisdom — for wisdom has more facets than you know.” Kiplayim lᵉṯûšiyyāh (double in sound wisdom) — kiplāyim (twofold/double) and tûšiyyāh (sound wisdom/abiding success/efficacy). Tûšiyyāh is a wisdom-technical term (Proverbs 2:7; Job 3:21; Job 8:14; Job 5:12; Job 12:16; Job 26:3) suggesting practical effectiveness that accomplishes its purpose. Zophar is saying: God has twice as much wisdom as you can see — implying Job’s suffering reveals a double portion of hidden divine wisdom-in-judgment that Job cannot access.
7: hăḥēqer ʾĕlôhah timṣāʾ ʾim ʾad-takkîṯ šadday timṣāʾ — “can you search out the deep things of God? Can you find the limit of the Almighty?” Ḥēqer (searching, limit) — “unsearchable” in the NT (anexeraunētos, Romans 11:33) — Zophar uses the vocabulary of divine incomprehensibility correctly but with the wrong pastoral application: the unsearchability of God should produce humility in the sufferer, not accusations of the sufferer’s guilt.
8: gāḇhê šāmayim māh-tipʿāl ʿᵃmuqqāh mišᵉʾôl māh-tēḏāʿ — “it is higher than the heavens — what can you do? Deeper than Sheol — what can you know?” The fourfold spatial description — higher than heaven, deeper than Sheol, longer than earth, broader than sea — is a cosmological mapping of divine incomprehensibility using all four dimensions: vertical up/down and horizontal. The quadrant imagery echoes Isaiah 40:12 (measuring the waters in the hollow of the hand).
9: ʾᵃrukāh mēʾereṣ midāḥ ûrᵉḥāḇāh min-yām — “its measure is longer than the earth and broader than the sea.” The horizontal dimensions complete the cosmic mapping. The accumulation of spatial impossibilities — God’s wisdom exceeds every human framework — is the same argument the whirlwind speech makes, but where Zophar uses it as accusation (you can’t know, therefore submit to punishment), YHWH uses it as invitation to awe and trust.
10: ʾim-yᵉḥᵃlōp wᵉyaśgîr wᵉyaqhîl mî yᵉšîḇennû — “if he sweeps through, imprisons, and convenes a court — who can stop him?” Three swift verbs of divine judicial action: yᵉḥᵃlōp (sweep through/pass by — same root as “pass by” in Job 9:11), yaśgîr (shut in/imprison), yaqhîl (convene an assembly/court). The swift judicial process allows no appeal; the rhetorical force parallels what Job said in Job 9:12 (“if he snatches away, who can stop him?”). Zophar and Job are using the same divine power-descriptions, but with different theological conclusions.
11: kî-hûʾ yēḏaʿ mᵉṯê-šāwᵉ — “for he knows worthless people.” Mᵉṯê-šāwᵉ (men of vanity/worthlessness) — šāwʾ carries the sense of deception, emptiness, falsehood; used in the third commandment (“do not take the name of the LORD in vain” — laššāwʾ). Zophar is effectively classifying Job in this category without saying it directly — the whole speech is an extended accusation with the final term withheld.
12: wᵉʾîš nāḇôḇ yillāḇēḇ wᵉʿayir pereʾ ʾāḏām yiwwālēḏ — “a hollow-headed man will become wise when a wild donkey gives birth to a human being.” Nāḇôḇ (hollow/empty) — the word for an empty vessel or reed; the hollow man becoming wise when a wild donkey gives birth to a human is an extreme adynaton (impossibility idiom) — the structural equivalent of “when pigs fly.” Zophar is saying Job’s becoming wise is as likely as this natural impossibility.
13: ʾim-ʾattāh hᵉḵînôṯā libbᵉḵā ûpāraśtā ʾēlāyw kappêḵā — “if you would prepare your heart and stretch out your hands toward him.” Hᵉḵînôṯā libbᵉḵā (prepare/establish your heart) — kûn in the hiphil means to make firm, establish, fix rightly; cf. Psalms 78:8 (their hearts not established/firm with God) and 1 Samuel 7:3 (prepare your hearts for the LORD). The stretching of hands (pāraś kappayim) is the gesture of prayer; Zophar’s conditional instruction is genuinely pious — but applied to an innocent man.
14: ʾim-ʾāwen bᵉyāḏᵉḵā harḥîqēhû wᵉʾal-tašken bᵉʾohāleḵā ʿawlāh — “if there is sin in your hand, remove it far away, and let wickedness have no place in your home.” Harḥîqēhû (remove it far) — the same idiom appears in Psalms 103:12 (as far as the east is from the west, so far has he removed our transgressions). Zophar’s advice is theologically correct but morally catastrophic: Job’s home has already been destroyed, his children dead; commanding him to remove wickedness from his household is a second wound.
15: kî-ʾāz tissāʾ pāneḵā mimmûm wᵉhāyîṯā môṣāq wᵉlōʾ ṯîrāʾ — “then surely you will lift up your face without shame; you will stand firm and have no fear.” The promise of restored dignity (lifting the face without shame) echoes the promise of Job 22:26 (Eliphaz) and the actual restoration in Job 42:8-10. The conditional “if you repent” will be fulfilled — but not because of Zophar’s diagnosis; it will be fulfilled because YHWH restores Job after the whirlwind, not after Zophar’s prescribed repentance.
16: kî-ʾattāh ʿāmāl tiškaḥ kᵉmayim ʿāḇrû tizkōr — “for you will forget your suffering; you will remember it only as water that has long since flowed on.” The water-memory image — suffering as rivers that pass and leave no trace — is a comforting image of selective forgetting; cf. Isaiah 65:16 (the former troubles will be forgotten). The promise of future forgetfulness will be partially fulfilled: Job 42:10-17 shows restoration, though the deaths of Job’s first children are never unfelt.
17: ûmissohᵃrayim yāqûm ḥeled ṯᵉʿupāh kaḇōqer yihyeh — “your life will shine brighter than noon; even your darkest time will be like dawn.” Light/darkness reversal as the metaphor for restoration from suffering; the darkest time becoming dawn inverts the Amos 5:20 judgment image (the day of the LORD: darkness not light, gloom not dawn). The restoration-as-light vocabulary will be fulfilled but through a very different path than Zophar envisions.
18: wᵉḇāṭaḥtā kî-yēš tiqwāh wᵉḥāpâartā lāḇeṭaḥ tiškaḇ — “you will have confidence, because there is hope; you will look around you and rest in security.” Yēš tiqwāh (there is hope) — the word tiqwāh appears again; Zophar is offering hope to the one whom he has accused of hopelessness. The pastoral form is correct; the theological premise (you are being punished for sin) is not.
19: wᵉrāḇaṣtā wᵉʾên maḥᵃrîḏ ûḥillû pāneḵā rabbîm — “you will lie down, and no one will make you afraid; many will come seeking your goodwill.” The reversal from isolation (Job has been abandoned by kinsmen and friends, Job 19:13-19) to being sought out is the restoration image. Ḥillû pāneḵā (seek your favor/caress your face) — a phrase for supplication; the restored Job will be the one to whom others come for favor and intercession, which is exactly what happens in Job 42:8-9 when the Friends must ask Job to pray for them.
20: wᵉʿênê rᵉšāʿîm tiklenāh wᵉmānôs ʾāḇaḏ minnêhem wᵉtiqwāṯām mappāḥ-nāpeš — “but the eyes of the wicked will fail; all means of escape will be closed to them, and their only hope is to breathe their last.” The climactic contrast — Job’s restored hope versus the wicked’s hopeless tiqwāh. Mappāḥ-nāpeš (the breathing out of the soul/the gasp of the dying) — the most bitter possible content of hope: the wicked’s only hope is to exhale their last breath. The grim wordplay on tiqwāh as both legitimate hope (Job 11:18) and the mere hope of death frames the chapter’s final irony.
